title: Popular Architectures
blurb: The canonical models: LeNet → AlexNet → VGG → ResNet (skip connections) → Inception, plus Autoencoders, U-Net, and the generative pair VAEs & GANs. Exams love the 'why' behind each design choice.

knowledge_points.title: CNN Architectures (LeNet, AlexNet, VGG)
knowledge_points.recap: **LeNet** — early CNN, uses average pooling & tanh/sigmoid. **AlexNet** — deeper, big $11\times11$ first filter, **ReLU** (enabling depth) + dropout. **VGG** — stacks of small $3\times3$ convs; the conv part is a feature extractor, FC layers at the end do classification. Paradigm: Conv→Pool…→Conv→Pool→FC…→FC; channels grow, spatial size shrinks with depth.

questions.q: LeNet uses average pooling. Give one advantage and one disadvantage vs max pooling. What is the receptive field of a neuron in LeNet's first FC layer?
questions.answer:
**Advantage** of avg pooling: every entry in the window receives a gradient. **Disadvantage:** it's a **linear** operation, while max pooling adds non-linearity.
**Receptive field of the first FC layer:** the **entire input image** ($32\times32$) — an FC layer connects to all positions, so it sees everything.
questions.extend: Any FC layer has a *global* receptive field by definition. Avg vs max pooling = linear/all-gradients vs non-linear/feature-selecting.

questions.q: AlexNet uses an $11\times11$ first-layer filter and ReLU instead of sigmoid/tanh. Name two disadvantages of such a large filter, and explain why ReLU (with Kaiming init) lets AlexNet be deeper.
questions.answer:
**Large $11\times11$ filter disadvantages:** (1) very **expensive** in parameters and computation; (2) captures **global** rather than fine **local** features.
**ReLU + Kaiming:** ReLU does not saturate for positive inputs, so it **prevents the vanishing-gradient problem** that sigmoid/tanh cause in deep nets — enabling AlexNet to train more layers than LeNet.
questions.extend: Modern nets replace one big filter with stacks of small $3\times3$ ones (VGG) — same receptive field, fewer params, more non-linearity. ReLU = the depth enabler.

questions.q: VGGNet: what is the purpose of the convolutional part of the model, and why do we still need the FC layers at the end?
questions.answer: The **convolutional part** acts as a **feature extractor** (local, hierarchical features). The **FC layers** have a **global receptive field**, which is what the final **classification** task needs to combine all features into class scores.
questions.extend: Conv = local features; FC head = global decision. This conv-backbone + FC-head split is the template most classifiers follow.

questions.q: What is true for common architectures like VGG-16 or LeNet?

options.text: The number of filters tends to increase as we go deeper into the network.
The width and height of the activation maps tend to increase with depth.
The input can be any size as long as width = height.
They follow Conv→Pool…→Conv→Pool→FC…→FC.

questions.answer: **True:** filter/channel count **increases** with depth, and they follow the **Conv→Pool→…→FC** paradigm. **False:** spatial size **decreases** (not increases) with depth, and the FC layers fix the input size (so arbitrary sizes are not allowed).
questions.extend: Standard CNN trend: spatial ↓, channels ↑ as you go deeper — trading resolution for semantic richness.

questions.q: Why did replacing sigmoid/tanh with ReLU (AlexNet) and stacking $3\times3$ convs (VGG) enable deeper networks than LeNet?
questions.answer: **ReLU** doesn't saturate for positive inputs → mitigates **vanishing gradients**, so deeper nets train. **Stacked $3\times3$ convs** grow the receptive field with fewer parameters and more non-linearities than big kernels. Together they made depth both trainable and efficient.
questions.extend: ReLU (gradient flow) + small-kernel stacks (efficiency) = the road to depth, later perfected by ResNet.

questions.q: Which changes were made from LeNet to AlexNet?
questions.answer: **True:** **using ReLU** as the activation, and **increasing depth and number of parameters**. **False:** skip connections (that's ResNet) and 'introducing convolutional layers' (LeNet already had them).
questions.extend: AlexNet's leap over LeNet: ReLU + more depth/params + dropout — not skip connections (those came with ResNet).

options.text: Adding skip connections.
Using ReLU as activation functions.
Introducing convolutional layers.
Increasing depth and number of parameters.

questions.q: A CNN does Conv2d(3,8)→Pool→BN→ReLU → Conv2d(8,16)→Pool→BN→ReLU → Conv2d(16,32)→Pool→BN→ReLU → Flatten → Linear(x,4). All conv: $k{=}3,p{=}1,s{=}1$; pools halve spatial size. (4.1) trainable params of the final Linear in terms of $x$; (4.4) params of the first conv Conv2d(3,8); (4.5) total params of all BatchNorm layers.
questions.answer: **4.1** Linear is $4\times x$ weights $+4$ bias $=\mathbf{4(x+1)}$. **4.4** $3\cdot3\cdot3\cdot8+8=216+8=\mathbf{224}$. **4.5** BN has 2 params per channel: $2(8+16+32)=\mathbf{112}$.
questions.extend: Conv params $=k^2 C_{in}C_{out}+C_{out}$; BN adds $2\times$channels; an FC layer adds out$\times$(in+1).

questions.q: For the above network with $80\times80$ RGB input, what is the flattened size $x$ into the Linear layer? Explain.
questions.answer: Each conv keeps spatial size ('same' padding) and each of the **3 max-pools halves** it: $80\to40\to20\to10$. Final depth is $32$, so $x=10\times10\times32=\mathbf{3200}$.
questions.extend: Track spatial size through pools (×½ each) and multiply by the last conv's channel count.

knowledge_points.title: Skip Connections & ResNet
knowledge_points.recap: A residual block computes $X_{l+1}=X_l+F(X_l)$. The identity path is a 'highway' for gradients: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial X_l}=\frac{\partial L}{\partial X_{l+1}}\big(1+\frac{\partial F(X_l)}{\partial X_l}\big)$ — the $+1$ lets the gradient bypass the block, mitigating vanishing gradients and enabling very deep nets.

questions.q: Why do skip connections create a 'highway of gradients'? Show it in the chain rule for $\frac{\partial L}{\partial X_l}$ given $X_{l+1}=X_l+F(X_l)$.
questions.answer:
Skip connections add the input to the output, so the gradient of that input can **bypass** the block instead of being diminished by it:
$\frac{\partial L}{\partial X_l}=\frac{\partial L}{\partial X_{l+1}}\frac{\partial X_{l+1}}{\partial X_l}=\frac{\partial L}{\partial X_{l+1}}\Big(\frac{\partial X_l}{\partial X_l}+\frac{\partial F(X_l)}{\partial X_l}\Big)=\frac{\partial L}{\partial X_{l+1}}\Big(1+\frac{\partial F(X_l)}{\partial X_l}\Big).$
The **$1$** guarantees gradient flow even if $\frac{\partial F}{\partial X_l}\to0$.
questions.extend: That additive $+1$ is the whole trick: the gradient is never fully attenuated, so 100+ layer ResNets train. Same idea reappears in LSTM cell state.

questions.q: Give a Python-like implementation of a residual block.
questions.answer:
```python
z = conv1(x)
z = relu(z)
z = conv2(z)
x = relu(x + z)   # add the input (skip connection), then activate
```
The key line is `x + z`: the block learns the residual $F(x)=z$ and adds it to the identity.
questions.extend: The convs must keep the spatial/channel shape so `x + z` aligns (use the $(3,1,1)$ trio, or a 1×1 conv on the skip to match channels).

questions.q: What is true about Residual Blocks?

options.text: Reduce the number of computations in the forward pass.
Act as a highway for gradient flow.
Enable a more stable training of larger networks.
Act as a regularizer.

questions.answer: **True:** residual blocks act as a **gradient highway** and **enable stable training of deeper networks**. They do **not** reduce forward computation (they add an addition), and are not primarily a regularizer.
questions.extend: Residual = solve vanishing gradients + degradation in deep nets. Their job is trainability of depth, not speed or regularization.

questions.q: A 56-layer plain CNN has HIGHER training error than a 20-layer one (the degradation problem). How do residual blocks fix this?
questions.answer: Residual blocks learn $F(x)$ and add the identity: $x+F(x)$. If the extra layers aren't useful they can learn $F(x)\approx0$, so a deeper net can **always match** a shallower one. The additive identity path also keeps gradients flowing (the $+1$ term).
questions.extend: Skip connections solve both vanishing gradients and the degradation problem of plain deep nets.

questions.q: For a residual block $H(x)=x+F(x)$ with $F=F_2(F_1(x))$, compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial x}$ and explain what happens when $\frac{\partial F}{\partial x}\to0$.
questions.answer: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial x}=\frac{\partial L}{\partial H}\big(1+\frac{\partial F}{\partial x}\big)$. When the inner gradients vanish ($\frac{\partial F}{\partial x}\to0$), $\frac{\partial L}{\partial x}\to\frac{\partial L}{\partial H}$ — the gradient **passes straight through the block** (the skip path), so it doesn't vanish.
questions.extend: The additive identity gives a guaranteed gradient highway: even if the block learns nothing, gradients still flow.

questions.q: Which statements about ResNet skip connections are true?
questions.answer: **True:** they add an identity path that lets gradients bypass a block, enabling very deep nets to train without the degradation problem. **False:** they don't change spatial resolution, and they don't remove the need for non-linearities inside the block.
questions.extend: Skip connection = additive identity path ⇒ gradient highway + trainable depth. Non-linearities stay inside $F(x)$.

options.text: They create an additive identity path for gradients.
They let very deep networks train without the degradation problem.
They increase the spatial resolution of feature maps.
They remove the need for any non-linearity in the block.

questions.q: (4.5) Name two problems when training very deep networks. (4.6) How do skip connections help, shown via a weight update for $w_0$ in $z=y+G(y)$ with $y=w_1(w_0x)$, $G(y)$ two more layers?
questions.answer: **4.5** Vanishing/exploding gradients (too many layers, chain rule), high compute / out-of-memory, and overfitting (high capacity). **4.6** Skip connections add an **identity path**: $\frac{dz}{dy}=\frac{d(y+G(y))}{dy}=1+\frac{dG}{dy}$, so $\frac{dz}{dw_0}=(1+\frac{dG}{dy})\,w_1x$. The **$+1$** guarantees the **full upstream gradient flows through** even if $\frac{dG}{dy}\to0$ — a gradient highway.
questions.extend: The additive identity gives $\partial z/\partial y=1+\partial G/\partial y$ → gradients never fully vanish.

knowledge_points.title: GoogLeNet / Inception
knowledge_points.recap: Inception runs multiple kernel sizes in parallel and concatenates them. The first version was too expensive (large kernels); **1×1 convolutions** were added to reduce channels before costly $3\times3/5\times5$ convs. MaxPool inside the block uses the size-preserving trio $(k{=}3,s{=}1,p{=}1)$ so its output can be concatenated.

questions.q: Inception: (i) what was the problem with the first version and how was it solved? (ii) MaxPool usually reduces spatial size — how was it used *inside* an Inception block and concatenated to the other outputs?
questions.answer:
(i) The first InceptionNet was **too complex/expensive** (large-kernel convolutions). Solved by inserting **1×1 convolutions** to **reduce the channel count** before the more expensive convs (a bottleneck).
(ii) By using the **size-preserving trio** $k{=}3,s{=}1,p{=}1$, the MaxPool keeps the spatial dimensions, so its output matches the others and can be **concatenated**.
questions.extend: 1×1 bottlenecks made 'going deeper' (GoogLeNet) affordable. The size-preserving trio is reused everywhere to keep parallel branches concatenable.

questions.q: Why does the Inception module use several kernel sizes ($1\times1, 3\times3, 5\times5$) in parallel instead of picking one?
questions.answer: Different kernel sizes capture features at **different scales/receptive fields**; running them in parallel and concatenating lets the network learn which scale matters at each location, rather than committing to a single fixed receptive field per layer.
questions.extend: Multi-scale in one layer = let the data choose the scale. The 1×1 bottlenecks keep this multi-branch design computationally feasible.

questions.q: Explain the Inception layer and list its internal elements.
questions.answer: **Idea:** apply **multiple kernel sizes in parallel** so the layer captures features at different scales. **Elements:** convolutions of different kernel sizes ($1\times1$, $3\times3$, $5\times5$), a **max-pooling** branch, and a **concatenation** of all branch outputs (with $1\times1$ convs as bottlenecks to cut cost).
questions.extend: Inception = parallel multi-scale convs + pooling, concatenated; 1×1 convs keep it cheap.

knowledge_points.title: Autoencoders
knowledge_points.recap: An autoencoder encodes input to a bottleneck $z$ then decodes back, trained with a **reconstruction** loss (MSE/MAE) — **unsupervised** (input is its own target). Latent too small → blurry/underfit; too big → learns identity/overfits. It is the non-linear analogue of PCA; great for unsupervised feature extraction on unlabeled data.

questions.q: For an image-reconstruction autoencoder: what loss can you use, what do you compare, and is it supervised or unsupervised? What linear method does it resemble and what's its advantage?
questions.answer:
**Loss:** MSE or MAE, comparing the **input** image with the **reconstructed** output. It is **unsupervised** (no labels — the input is the target).
**Resembles PCA** (linear dimensionality reduction); the autoencoder's advantage is that it is **non-linear** and can learn more complex features.
questions.extend: Autoencoder = non-linear PCA. 'No ground-truth labels needed' is the recurring exam phrase (also Ch I, SS22 5.3).

questions.q: What happens if the autoencoder's latent space (bottleneck) is too small? Too big?
questions.answer:
**Too small:** too much information is lost → poor/blurry reconstruction → **underfitting**.
**Too big:** the network can learn to **copy** the input (identity mapping) without learning meaningful features → **overfitting** / no useful compression.
questions.extend: The bottleneck size is the key hyperparameter — it forces a trade-off between compression (small) and reconstruction fidelity (large).

questions.q: Give a scenario where you'd use an autoencoder for feature extraction.
questions.answer: When you have **lots of unlabeled data** and little labeled data: train the autoencoder unsupervised to reconstruct the unlabeled data, then reuse the **encoder** as a pre-trained **feature extractor** for a supervised task (attach a small classifier, fine-tune on the labeled set).
questions.extend: This is the transfer-learning recipe of Ch IV: unsupervised pre-training → supervised fine-tuning, with the autoencoder encoder as backbone.

questions.q: Which statements about Autoencoders are true?

options.text: Only usable for image data, not other data types.
Autoencoders can be used for dimensionality reduction.
Autoencoders require labeled training data.
Autoencoders can be trained without a loss function.

questions.answer: Only **dimensionality reduction** is true. Autoencoders work on any data type, are **unsupervised** (no labels), and need a **reconstruction loss** to train.
questions.extend: Bottleneck = compressed code → dimensionality reduction. 'Self-supervised': the label is the input itself.

questions.q: How would you turn a plain autoencoder into an anomaly detector?
questions.answer: Train the autoencoder **only on normal data** so it reconstructs normal inputs well. At test time, inputs with **high reconstruction error** are flagged as anomalies — the model can't reconstruct patterns it never learned.
questions.extend: Reconstruction error as an anomaly score — a classic unsupervised use of autoencoders.

questions.q: Which statements about autoencoders are true?
questions.answer: **True:** an autoencoder **can be used as a lossy compressor** (bottleneck code). **False:** the hidden layer should be **smaller** (not larger) than the input; encoder/decoder need not have equal layer counts; and not *any* autoencoder can perfectly learn the identity (a tight bottleneck can't).
questions.extend: AE = lossy compression via a bottleneck; making the latent ≥ input invites the trivial identity / no compression.

options.text: The hidden layer should ideally be larger than the input layer.
The encoder and decoder have the same number of layers.
Any autoencoder can perfectly learn the identity function.
An autoencoder can be used as a lossy compressor.

questions.q: Explain the primary purpose of an autoencoder (3.6). What are its two main components and the role of each (3.7)?
questions.answer: **Purpose:** compress the input into a **lower-dimensional (bottleneck) representation** and **reconstruct** it, minimizing reconstruction error → dimensionality reduction / feature extraction. **Components:** the **Encoder** compresses the input, capturing the essential information as a latent code; the **Decoder** reconstructs the original input from that code.
questions.extend: Encoder ↓ to bottleneck, Decoder ↑ back to input; the bottleneck forces a compact representation.

questions.q: How can you modify an autoencoder to perform classification (1p)? What difference does pretraining the autoencoder on the same dataset before fine-tuning make (1p)?
questions.answer: **Modification:** replace the **decoder** with a **fully-connected classification head** on top of the encoder's latent code. **Pretraining effect:** the encoder learns **good latent representations**, so the classifier **converges faster (and potentially better)** during fine-tuning.
questions.extend: Encoder-as-feature-extractor + new head is the standard AE→classifier recipe; pretraining warm-starts the encoder.

questions.q: How do the encoder, code, and decoder of an autoencoder each function?
questions.answer: **Encoder:** performs **dimensionality reduction**, mapping the input to a compact representation. **Code:** the **compressed, low-dimensional latent** representation (bottleneck). **Decoder:** **reconstructs** the original-dimension input from the latent code (a lossy reconstruction).
questions.extend: Encoder↓ → code (bottleneck) → decoder↑; the code is the learned compression.

questions.q: You have a small labeled dataset for semantic segmentation plus a large unlabeled image dataset. How can an autoencoder help?
questions.answer: **Pretrain** an autoencoder to **reconstruct** the inputs on the **whole (unlabeled) dataset**, so its encoder learns useful features. Then take the **trained encoder** and fine-tune it with the **labeled data** to train the segmentation model.
questions.extend: Self-supervised pretraining: learn features from unlabeled data, then transfer the encoder to the labeled task.

questions.q: Your trained autoencoder produces blurry reconstructions. Name and explain two possible reasons.
questions.answer: **MSE/L2 loss:** its optimum is the **average** of plausible outputs, which **blurs** high-frequency detail. **Latent dimension too small:** the bottleneck has **insufficient capacity** to represent the data, losing information → blur.
questions.extend: Blurry AE output ⇒ averaging loss (MSE) and/or an over-tight bottleneck.

questions.q: You have a trained autoencoder on many product images and few prototype images. Build a 'prototype vs not' classifier; give the loss and number of output neurons.
questions.answer: **Transfer learning:** reuse the **pretrained encoder** as a feature extractor, **replace the decoder with a classifier head**, and fine-tune on the small prototype set. For binary classification: **BCE loss with 1 output neuron** (or cross-entropy with 2).
questions.extend: Pretrained encoder + small classifier head = data-efficient transfer; binary head = sigmoid+BCE.

knowledge_points.title: U-Net & Fully Convolutional Networks
knowledge_points.recap: U-Net is an encoder–decoder with **skip connections** between matching encoder/decoder levels, preserving fine spatial detail lost during downsampling. It accepts variable input sizes (fully convolutional) and processes most computation at low resolution. Channel count is increased to compensate for reduced spatial size.

questions.q: Give advantages of U-Net over a vanilla autoencoder. How do we mitigate the drop in spatial size so not too much information is lost?
questions.answer:
**Advantages:** (i) **skip connections** between encoder and decoder preserve fine detail; (ii) most processing happens at **lower resolution** (cheaper); (iii) accepts **different input sizes** with no structural change; (iv) extracts meaningful **local** features instead of only global ones.
**Mitigation:** **increase the number of channels** as spatial size shrinks, giving features more 'breathing room' to pass through.
questions.extend: Skip connections in U-Net carry high-resolution detail across the bottleneck — crucial for segmentation where pixel-precise boundaries matter.

questions.q: For plain image *reconstruction*, does it make sense to use encoder–decoder skip connections? Explain.
questions.answer: **No** — for pure reconstruction, skip connections let the model **copy** the input straight to the output (memorize/identity), so it never learns a meaningful compressed representation. Skip connections help **segmentation** (need detail) but defeat the purpose of a compressing autoencoder.
questions.extend: Skip connections + reconstruction target = trivial identity shortcut. They're beneficial only when the output needs the bypassed high-res detail (segmentation).

questions.q: Why are U-Net's encoder→decoder skip connections crucial for segmentation but harmful for a compressing autoencoder?
questions.answer: For **segmentation**, the skips carry high-resolution spatial detail (lost in downsampling) to the decoder for precise boundaries. For a **compressing autoencoder**, the skips let the model copy the input across the bottleneck (an identity shortcut), so it never learns a compressed representation.
questions.extend: Same mechanism, opposite verdict — it depends on whether you WANT the bypassed high-res detail.

questions.q: Which statements about upsampling are true?
questions.answer: **True:** **transposed convolution adds trainable parameters** whereas interpolation does not; and **transposed convolution ≈ unpooling followed by a standard convolution**. The other options (conv∘transposed-conv = identity; transposed-conv = bilinear-interp + conv) are false.
questions.extend: Learned upsampling (transposed conv) has parameters; interpolation (nearest/bilinear) does not.

options.text: A convolution then a transposed convolution with identical weights is an identity operation.
Transposed convolution adds trainable parameters, whereas interpolation does not.
Transposed convolution is equivalent to unpooling followed by a standard convolution.
Transposed convolution equals bilinear interpolation followed by a standard convolution.

questions.q: Describe the two steps involved in an upconvolution (transposed convolution).
questions.answer: In order: **(1) upsample** the input by inserting zeros between elements (intermediate zero-padding / unpooling); **(2)** apply a **regular convolution** over the upsampled feature map. (Calling it 'transposed convolution' is only accepted if broken down into these two steps.)
questions.extend: Upconv = zero-insertion upsampling **then** an ordinary conv; order matters.

questions.q: If you use a U-Net for semantic segmentation, what is a skip connection in U-Net?
questions.answer: A **direct concatenation** from an **encoder** layer to the **decoder** layer at the same resolution. It passes **fine-grained spatial features** to the decoder and acts as a **gradient highway** (helps avoid vanishing gradients), improving localization.
questions.extend: U-Net skips concatenate encoder→decoder features (not residual adds), restoring spatial detail lost at the bottleneck.

questions.q: An autoencoder decoder uses Transposed Convolution. (1) A simple alternative? (2) Why might transposed conv outperform it? (3) What artifact appears if kernel size isn't divisible by the stride?
questions.answer: (1) **Upsampling / interpolation** (nearest-neighbor or bilinear resize, then conv). (2) Transposed conv has **learnable parameters**, so it can learn the data-specific upsampling structure. (3) **Checkerboard artifacts** from uneven kernel overlap.
questions.extend: Learned upsampling beats fixed interpolation but risks checkerboard artifacts when stride ∤ kernel.

questions.q: What is the main structural difference of U-Net vs a conventional CNN autoencoder, and why does it improve reconstruction?
questions.answer: **Skip connections** linking encoder layers directly to the matching decoder layers (passing pre-bottleneck features). They let **high-frequency spatial detail flow directly to the output** (better localization/detail) and provide a **gradient shortcut** for training deeper layers.
questions.extend: U-Net's encoder→decoder skips restore fine detail lost at the bottleneck and ease optimization.

questions.q: You distribute only the decoder; clients send you the latent vector. Is a vanilla fully-conv autoencoder or a fully-conv U-Net better, and why?
questions.answer: **Vanilla autoencoder.** A **U-Net has skip connections from encoder to decoder**, so the decoder needs the encoder's intermediate features — but clients only have the **decoder and the latent vector**, not those features. A plain AE decodes from the latent alone.
questions.extend: U-Net skips require encoder activations at decode time → unusable when only the decoder + code are shared.

knowledge_points.title: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)
knowledge_points.recap: VAEs learn the data distribution **explicitly**. Two losses: a **reconstruction** loss (MSE/MAE) + a **KL-divergence** term forcing the latent toward a standard normal. The **reparameterization trick** $z=\mu+\sigma\epsilon$ (with $\epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$) makes sampling differentiable so backprop works.

questions.q: VAE: what two losses are used and their purpose? What distribution do we assume for the latent? How does the reparameterization trick enable sampling?
questions.answer:
**Two losses:** (1) **reconstruction** (MAE/MSE) to match the output to the input; (2) **KL divergence** to push the latent distribution toward the assumed prior.
**Assumed distribution:** the **standard normal** $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$.
**Reparameterization trick:** sample $z=\mu+\sigma\epsilon$ with $\epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ — the randomness is moved into $\epsilon$, so $\mu,\sigma$ stay differentiable and gradients can flow (you can't backprop through a raw random sample).
questions.extend: KL keeps the latent space smooth/continuous so you can sample & interpolate; the reparam trick is what makes the stochastic node trainable by backprop.

questions.q: What is the difference between how GANs and VAEs learn the training-set distribution?
questions.answer: **VAEs** learn the distribution **explicitly (directly)** — they model $p(x)$ via an encoder/decoder and a KL-regularized latent. **GANs** learn it **implicitly (indirectly)** — the generator learns to produce samples that fool a discriminator, never writing down $p(x)$.
questions.extend: Explicit (VAE) → smoother, blurrier samples, tractable latent. Implicit (GAN) → sharper samples, harder/unstable training.

questions.q: Why can't a plain autoencoder generate new images by sampling random latent vectors, while a VAE can?
questions.answer: A plain AE's latent space is **unstructured / has gaps**, so a random vector decodes to garbage. A VAE's **KL term** forces the latent toward a known prior $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$, making it **continuous and structured**; you can then sample $z\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ and decode realistic images (the reparameterization trick makes this trainable).
questions.extend: KL regularization + reparameterization = a smooth, sampleable latent space.

questions.q: What are the main differences between a standard autoencoder and a variational autoencoder?
questions.answer: A **standard AE** imposes **no structure on the latent space** — it only compresses and reconstructs the input (reconstruction loss only). A **VAE imposes a prior on the latent space** (learns a probability distribution), **samples from the latent distribution**, and adds a **KL-divergence term** to the loss → enabling generation of new samples.
questions.extend: VAE = AE + a regularized, samplable latent (KL to a prior). Plain AE's latent has 'holes', so it can't generate.

questions.q: What are the differences between an autoencoder and a variational autoencoder in terms of goal and loss?
questions.answer: **Goal:** AE learns an efficient embedding to **reconstruct** the input; VAE learns a **latent distribution** so it can **generate new data** from the same distribution. **Loss:** AE uses a **reconstruction loss** (MSE/L1); VAE uses **reconstruction + KL-divergence** (forcing the latent toward a Gaussian).
questions.extend: VAE = AE + a probabilistic, samplable latent regularized by KL; plain AE only reconstructs.

questions.q: Why is a VAE better than a plain AE for generating new, similar product images? What architecture change and loss are needed?
questions.answer: A **VAE enforces a probability distribution** (Gaussian) on the latent space, so you can **sample** new latents and decode realistic new images (a plain AE's latent has gaps). **Change:** the encoder outputs a **mean and variance** (not a fixed vector). **Loss:** **reconstruction + KL-divergence**.
questions.extend: VAE's regularized, samplable latent enables generation; encoder→(μ,σ), loss = recon + KL.

knowledge_points.title: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)
knowledge_points.recap: A GAN pits a **generator** (turns noise into fake images) against a **discriminator** (real vs fake), trained min-max. Discriminator loss is BCE on real(1)/fake(0); to train the generator we relabel fakes as **1** to fool D. Prone to **underfitting** and **mode collapse** (generator outputs one image that fools D). GANs give sharper images than VAEs but are unstable to train.

questions.q: In the vanilla GAN, why is it prone to underfitting and mode collapse? How do we train the generator to fool the discriminator?
questions.answer:
**Underfitting / mode collapse:** generator and discriminator work **against each other** in a min-max game, so both can converge to **suboptimal** solutions; the generator may learn to produce a **single image** that reliably fools the discriminator (mode collapse), ignoring diversity.
**Training the generator:** pass the fake image through the discriminator but label it as **class 1 (real)**, so the generator is pushed to make fakes the discriminator scores as real.
questions.extend: The 'relabel fakes as real' is the generator's objective. Mode collapse = generator exploits one D-fooling sample; WGAN/conditional GAN variants address instability.

questions.q: What is true about Generative Adversarial Networks?

options.text: The Generator minimizes the probability that the Discriminator is correct.
The Generator provides supervision for the Discriminator.
The Discriminator acts as a classifier.
The Discriminator samples from a latent space.

questions.answer: **True:** the generator **minimizes the probability the discriminator is correct** (fools it), and the **discriminator is a classifier** (real vs fake). **False:** the *real data* (not the generator) supervises D, and it's the **generator** that samples from the latent space, not the discriminator.
questions.extend: Roles: G = noise→image (samples latent); D = image→real/fake (classifier). They train adversarially in a min-max game.

questions.q: Write the discriminator and generator losses of a vanilla GAN.
questions.answer:
**Discriminator (BCE, real=1/fake=0):** $L_D=-\frac{1}{2N}\sum_i\big(y_i\log D(x_i)+(1-y_i)\log(1-D(G(z_i)))\big)$.
**Generator (relabel fakes as real):** $L_G=-\frac{1}{N}\sum_i\log D(G(z_i))$ — maximize the discriminator's probability that the fake is real.
questions.extend: D wants $D(\text{real})\to1, D(\text{fake})\to0$; G wants $D(\text{fake})\to1$. The two losses pull D's output on fakes in opposite directions.

questions.q: During GAN training the discriminator becomes near-perfect early (D≈1 on real, ≈0 on fake). Why is this a problem for the generator?
questions.answer: A near-perfect discriminator gives the generator **vanishing gradients** (its loss saturates), so the generator **stops learning**. Remedies: balance D/G training, use a **non-saturating or Wasserstein** loss, or weaken D.
questions.extend: GAN training needs a balanced adversary; an over-strong D starves G of gradient — a core GAN instability.

questions.q: Which statements about GANs are true?
questions.answer: **True:** the **generator maximizes the probability the discriminator labels its output 'real'**, and after training the **discriminator loss tends to a constant** (it can no longer tell real from fake). **False:** the discriminator does not model the input distribution explicitly, and it cannot be used as a sampler (the generator samples).
questions.extend: G fools D (relabel fakes as real); at equilibrium D≈0.5 everywhere (constant loss). Sampling is the generator's role.

options.text: The generator maximizes the probability its output is classified 'real' by the discriminator.
The discriminator learns the distribution of input images, but the generator does not.
After training, the discriminator loss tends to a constant value.
A fully trained discriminator can be used as a sampling mechanism.

questions.q: How do an autoencoder and a GAN differ in network architecture (0.5p each)? What is the goal of the discriminator loss in a GAN (1p)?
questions.answer: **Architecture:** an **AE has an encoder** (+decoder, trained cooperatively); a **GAN has a discriminator** (+generator, trained adversarially). **Discriminator loss goal:** to **correctly classify real vs fake** images — pushing the generator to make outputs that look real.
questions.extend: AE: encoder–decoder, cooperative; GAN: generator–discriminator, adversarial.
